message:
feat(daemon): implement Prometheus metrics endpoint (process_triage-0493.1)

Add /metrics HTTP endpoint exposing daemon operational metrics in
Prometheus exposition format:

- Counters: pt_scans_total, pt_escalations_total, pt_errors_total
- Gauges: pt_daemon_uptime_seconds, pt_tick_count, pt_load_average,
  pt_memory_used_mb, pt_process_count, pt_orphan_count, etc.
- Histograms: pt_scan_duration_seconds, pt_tick_duration_seconds
- Info: pt_build_info with version metadata

HTTP server runs on configurable port (default 9184, localhost only)
using tiny_http. Includes /health endpoint and graceful shutdown.

Feature-gated behind `metrics` feature flag with prometheus + tiny_http
dependencies. 8 unit tests including live server integration test.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"process_triage-02t7","title":"Implement infinite mixture models (DPMM/IMM) for process clustering","description":"## Section 4.12 - Infinite Mixture Models

**Purpose**: Cluster processes into behavioral groups without pre-specifying K. New process types (novel test frameworks, new agents) should spawn new clusters automatically.

**Mathematical Background**:
- Dirichlet Process: G ~ DP(α, G_0) where α is concentration, G_0 is base measure
- Chinese Restaurant Process: P(new table) = α/(n+α), P(table k) = n_k/(n+α)
- Stick-breaking: π_k = V_k Π_{j<k}(1-V_j) where V_k ~ Beta(1, α)
- Collapsed Gibbs: Integrate out mixture weights, sample assignments
- Variational inference: Mean-field approximation for scalability

**Implementation Requirements**:
1. `DPMM::new(alpha, base_distribution)` - Initialize with concentration
2. `DPMM::fit(data, iterations)` - Collapsed Gibbs sampler
3. `DPMM::predict(new_point)` - Posterior predictive cluster assignment
4. `DPMM::num_clusters()` - Effective number of clusters (α-dependent)

**Why This Matters for pt**:
We don't know how many 'types' of processes exist. DPMM learns this. If a new AI agent (say 'gemini') appears, it forms its own cluster rather than being forced into 'claude' or 'codex' cluster.

**Integration Points**:
- Process classification (Section 2.2)
- Prior elicitation (Section 2.3)
- Pattern library extension (Section 3.9)

**Test Requirements**:
- Verify cluster recovery on synthetic mixture data
- Verify concentration parameter affects cluster count correctly
- Verify new clusters form for truly novel data","notes":"Duplicate/out-of-tree bead.  {"id":"process_triage-03n","title":"Implement evidence ledger display with drill-down","description":"## Overview
Create an interactive evidence ledger display allowing users to explore all signals collected for each process.

## Background
The inference engine collects dozens of evidence signals per process: CPU usage patterns, memory trends, file descriptors, network connections, cgroup membership, etc. Users need to see this evidence to understand and verify recommendations.

## Why It Matters
Trust comes from transparency. When pt says a process is likely abandoned, users should be able to drill down and see exactly what signals contributed to that conclusion. This also helps identify false positives by revealing which evidence might be misleading.

## Technical Approach
1. Organize evidence by category (resources, timing, context, behavior)
2. Show evidence items with their Bayesian weight contribution
3. Implement drill-down from summary to detail view
4. Support filtering by evidence type or strength
5. Highlight strongest contributors (highest Bayes factors)

## Display Components
- **Summary row**: Top 3 contributors with abbreviated values
- **Category panels**: Expandable sections for each evidence type
- **Detail view**: Full signal value, prior, likelihood, posterior contribution
- **Search/filter**: Find specific evidence by name or value

## Evidence Categories
- Resource usage (CPU, memory, IO)
- Timing (age, idle time, burst patterns)
- Context (parent, cgroup, user)
- Behavior (signals, restarts, file activity)
- Network (connections, listening ports)

## Success Criteria
- All evidence visible and organized
- Drill-down navigation intuitive
- Search and filter functional
- Bayes factor contributions displayed accurately

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-0493","title":"EPIC: Observability Integration (Prometheus, OpenTelemetry)","description":"## Overview
Integrate with modern observability stack for monitoring pt daemon and exposing metrics for external consumption.

## Background
Production deployments need observability. The dormant daemon (ptd) should expose metrics and traces for integration with existing monitoring infrastructure.

## Scope

### 1. Prometheus Metrics Endpoint
- \`/metrics\` HTTP endpoint (configurable port, default 9184)
- Metrics:
  - \`pt_scan_duration_seconds\`: Histogram of scan times (buckets: 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 30, 60)
  - \`pt_candidates_total\`: Gauge of current candidates by class
  - \`pt_kills_total\`: Counter of kills by outcome (success/failed/rollback)
  - \`pt_daemon_cpu_percent\`: Daemon resource usage
  - \`pt_daemon_memory_bytes\`: Daemon memory usage
  - \`pt_trigger_events_total\`: Counter of escalation triggers by type
  - \`pt_false_positive_rate\`: Gauge from shadow mode calibration
- Labels: host, user, classification, action_type

### 2. OpenTelemetry Tracing
- Span per scan/inference/action
- Trace context propagation for agent calls
- Exporters: Jaeger, OTLP (gRPC and HTTP), stdout
- Baggage: session_id, agent_name, host_id
- Sampling: Head-based with configurable rate

### 3. Structured Logging (JSON-L)
- Already implemented in telemetry lake
- Ensure log shipping compatibility (fluentd, vector, filebeat)
- Log format: OTLP-compatible JSON
- Correlation: trace_id in log records

### 4. Health Check Endpoint
- \`/healthz\` for liveness (always 200 if running)
- \`/readyz\` for readiness (200 if scan possible)
- Daemon status in JSON: version, uptime, last_scan, config_hash

### 5. Grafana Dashboard Template
- Pre-built dashboard JSON for common metrics
- Alerting rules (Prometheus alertmanager format)
- Documentation for import

---

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/src/observability/metrics_test.rs\`
- **Coverage target**: 90% for metrics emission
- Test cases:
  - Histogram bucket calculations
  - Counter increment correctness
  - Label cardinality limits
  - Metric registry isolation

### Integration Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/tests/observability_integration.rs\`
- Test cases:
  - \`/metrics\` endpoint serves valid Prometheus format
  - \`/healthz\` returns 200
  - \`/readyz\` returns appropriate status
  - OTLP exporter connects to mock collector
  - Trace context propagates through call chain

### E2E Tests
- **File**: \`test/observability_e2e.bats\`
- Test scenarios:
  - Start daemon, scrape \`/metrics\`, validate format
  - Trigger scan, verify metrics update
  - Send traces to local Jaeger, query via API
  - Prometheus can scrape endpoint and store metrics
- **Artifact logging**: Prometheus scrape logs, trace JSON exports

### Load Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/benches/metrics_overhead.rs\`
- Verify metrics emission adds < 1ms per operation
- Verify memory overhead < 5MB for metrics

---

## Logging Specifications

### Structured Log Events
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`metrics.server_start\` | INFO | port, path | Metrics server startup |
| \`metrics.scrape\` | TRACE | client_ip, duration_ms | Scrape request |
| \`trace.span_start\` | TRACE | trace_id, span_id, operation | Span started |
| \`trace.span_end\` | TRACE | trace_id, span_id, duration_ms, status | Span ended |
| \`health.check\` | DEBUG | endpoint, status, checks | Health check |

### Metric Naming Conventions
- Prefix: \`pt_\`
- Suffix: \`_total\` for counters, \`_seconds\` for durations, \`_bytes\` for sizes
- Labels: lowercase, underscore-separated

---

## Error Handling

### Failure Modes
| Scenario | Detection | Recovery | User Message |
|----------|-----------|----------|--------------|
| Port in use | bind() EADDRINUSE | Try next port or fail | "Metrics port 9184 in use. Try --metrics-port=9185" |
| OTLP unreachable | export fails | Buffer locally, retry | "OTLP endpoint unreachable. Buffering traces locally." |
| High cardinality | label count check | Drop labels | "High cardinality detected. Dropping low-value labels." |

### Graceful Degradation
1. No OTLP endpoint → disable tracing (metrics still work)
2. Prometheus scrape fails → log warning, keep serving
3. Buffer overflow → drop oldest traces

---

## Performance Targets
- Metrics endpoint latency: < 10ms for scrape
- Metrics overhead: < 0.1% CPU
- Trace export: async, non-blocking
- Memory for metrics: < 5MB baseline

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Prometheus metrics endpoint works
- [ ] Standard metrics cover key operations
- [ ] OTLP traces exported correctly
- [ ] Health endpoints respond correctly
- [ ] Documentation for Grafana/Prometheus setup
- [ ] Grafana dashboard template provided
- [ ] Unit tests pass with 90%+ coverage
- [ ] E2E tests pass in CI
- [ ] Load tests verify overhead targets

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Dormant daemon (process_triage-b4v)
- Optional: OpenTelemetry SDK for Rust (tracing-opentelemetry)
- Depends on: Testing infrastructure (process_triage-aii)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T18:52:52.709022643Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-16T20:26:00.439980735Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-0493","depends_on_id":"process_triage-b4v","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:54Z","created_by":"import"}]}
-{"id":"process_triage-0493.1","title":"Implement Prometheus metrics endpoint for daemon","description":"## Overview
Implement Prometheus metrics endpoint for the dormant daemon (ptd).

## Background
Production deployments need monitoring. The \`/metrics\` endpoint exposes key operational metrics in Prometheus format.

## Scope

### 1. Metrics Server
- HTTP server on configurable port (default: 9184)
- Path: \`/metrics\` (Prometheus standard)
- Concurrent: Handle multiple scrapers
- Minimal resource overhead

### 2. Metrics Definitions
**Counters:**
- \`pt_scans_total{status}\`: Total scans (success/failed)
- \`pt_kills_total{outcome,classification}\`: Kills by outcome and class
- \`pt_escalations_total{type}\`: Escalation events
- \`pt_errors_total{type}\`: Errors by type

**Gauges:**
- \`pt_candidates_current{classification}\`: Current candidate count by class
- \`pt_daemon_uptime_seconds\`: Daemon uptime
- \`pt_last_scan_timestamp\`: Unix timestamp of last scan
- \`pt_wealth_current\`: Alpha investing wealth level
- \`pt_fdr_estimate\`: Current FDR estimate

**Histograms:**
- \`pt_scan_duration_seconds\`: Scan duration (buckets: 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 30, 60)
- \`pt_inference_duration_seconds\`: Inference time per process

**Info:**
- \`pt_build_info{version,commit,build_date}\`: Build information

### 3. Labels
- \`host\`: Hostname (configurable, may be hashed)
- \`classification\`: useful, useful_bad, abandoned, zombie
- \`status\`: success, failed, skipped
- \`outcome\`: confirmed, rolled_back, failed

### 4. Configuration
- \`--metrics-port PORT\`: Metrics server port
- \`--metrics-path PATH\`: Custom path (default: /metrics)
- \`--metrics-labels KEY=VAL\`: Additional labels
- \`--no-metrics\`: Disable metrics server

### 5. Security
- Bind to localhost by default
- \`--metrics-bind ADDR\`: Custom bind address
- No authentication (rely on network security)
- Rate limiting: 10 scrapes/second

---

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/src/observability/prometheus_test.rs\`
- **Coverage target**: 90% for metrics emission
- Test cases:
  - Counter increment correctness
  - Gauge set/update correctness
  - Histogram observation and bucket calculation
  - Label validation (no high cardinality)
  - Metric registration (no duplicates)
  - Info metric generation

### Integration Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/tests/prometheus_integration.rs\`
- Test cases:
  - Server starts and serves /metrics
  - Metrics update after daemon operations
  - Multiple concurrent scrapes work
  - Custom port and path work
  - Rate limiting enforced

### E2E Tests
- **File**: \`test/prometheus_e2e.bats\`
- Test scenarios:
  - Start daemon with metrics, curl /metrics, validate format
  - Run scan, verify pt_scans_total incremented
  - Prometheus can scrape endpoint (real Prometheus in Docker)
  - Metrics persist across scrapes (counters don't reset)
- **Artifact logging**: Metrics snapshots before/after operations

### Load Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/benches/prometheus_bench.rs\`
- Test cases:
  - 1000 metric updates/second: latency < 1ms
  - 100 concurrent scrapes: no errors
  - Memory stable under continuous scraping

---

## Logging Specifications

### Structured Log Events
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`metrics.server_start\` | INFO | port, path, bind | Server started |
| \`metrics.scrape\` | TRACE | client_ip, duration_ms | Scrape handled |
| \`metrics.rate_limit\` | WARN | client_ip | Rate limit hit |
| \`metrics.register\` | DEBUG | metric_name, type | Metric registered |
| \`metrics.error\` | ERROR | error_message | Metrics error |

---

## Error Handling

### Failure Modes
| Scenario | Detection | Recovery | User Message |
|----------|-----------|----------|--------------|
| Port in use | bind() EADDRINUSE | Exit with error | "Metrics port 9184 in use. Use --metrics-port to change." |
| Rate limit | Counter exceeded | Return 429 | (HTTP 429 Too Many Requests) |
| Metric registration conflict | Prometheus error | Panic (dev bug) | (Crash with backtrace) |

### Graceful Handling
- Metrics server failure doesn't crash daemon
- Metrics unavailable logged as warning
- Scrape errors return 500 with details

---

## Performance Targets
- Scrape latency: < 10ms for 100 metrics
- Memory overhead: < 5MB for metrics
- CPU overhead: < 0.1% idle
- No metric update blocking daemon

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] \`/metrics\` endpoint serves Prometheus format
- [ ] All specified metrics implemented
- [ ] Labels follow best practices
- [ ] Configurable port and path
- [ ] Localhost bind by default
- [ ] Rate limiting works
- [ ] Unit tests pass with 90%+ coverage
- [ ] E2E tests with real Prometheus pass
- [ ] Load tests show acceptable performance

## Dependencies
- Part of: Observability epic (process_triage-0493)
- Depends on: Dormant daemon (process_triage-b4v)
- Library: prometheus crate for Rust","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T20:01:16.748276002Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-16T20:32:43.564673271Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-0493.1","depends_on_id":"process_triage-0493","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:54Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"process_triage-097","title":"Self-Update Mechanism","description":"## Overview
Add a secure, robust self-update mechanism to pt, following the patterns established in repo_updater and giil.

## Current State
- pt has NO self-update capability
- Users must manually check for updates
- No VERSION file for version tracking
- No infrastructure for update verification

## Target State
A complete self-update system with:
1. **Version detection** via HTTP redirect (no GitHub API needed)
2. **Checksum verification** for security
3. **Bash syntax validation** before replacement
4. **Atomic file replacement** to prevent corruption
5. **Permission pre-checking** for fail-fast behavior

## Why No GitHub API?
From repo_updater's design:
- Avoids rate limiting issues
- Works through corporate proxies that block API
- Simpler implementation with fewer failure modes
- Uses redirect probing: curl follows `/releases/latest` → extracts version from final URL

## Security Model
1. Download checksums.txt from GitHub release
2. Verify downloaded script matches expected hash
3. Validate bash syntax with `bash -n`
4. Only then replace the running script

## Implementation Pattern
```bash
# Get latest version without API
latest_url=$(curl -fsSL -o /dev/null -w '%{url_effective}' \
    'https://github.com/USER/REPO/releases/latest')
latest_version=${latest_url##*/v}  # Extract version from URL

# Verify checksum
expected=$(curl -fsSL ".../checksums.sha256" | grep ' pt$' | cut -d' ' -f1)
actual=$(sha256sum "$temp_file" | cut -d' ' -f1)
[[ "$expected" == "$actual" ]] || die 'Checksum mismatch'

# Validate syntax before replacing
bash -n "$temp_file" || die 'Downloaded file is not valid bash'

# Atomic replacement
mv "$temp_file" "$script_path"
```

## CLI Integration
- `pt update` - Check and install updates
- `pt update --check` - Check only, don't install
- `pt --version` - Show current version (already exists)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] VERSION file exists and is single source of truth
- [ ] Update check works without GitHub API
- [ ] Checksums are verified before installation
- [ ] Syntax is validated before replacement
- [ ] Atomic replacement prevents corruption
- [ ] Clear messaging for all outcomes","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T03:31:19.148532987Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T15:41:04.467613664Z","closed_at":"2026-01-15T15:41:04.467613664Z","close_reason":"All self-update mechanism child tasks completed (pt update, version/checksum/syntax/atomic replace).","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-097","depends_on_id":"process_triage-h89","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:54Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-0gtc","title":"Implement DRO worst-case loss gating","description":"## Overview
Implement Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) for conservative decisions under distribution shift.

## From Plan Sections 4.42, 5.12

### Mathematical Foundation
Replace expected loss with worst-case expected loss over an ambiguity set.

**Wasserstein DRO**:
```
sup_{Q: W(Q,P) ≤ ε} E_Q[L]
```

Where:
- P: Empirical/baseline distribution
- Q: Adversarial distribution in Wasserstein ball
- ε: Ambiguity radius (how much shift we protect against)
- L: Loss function

### Dual Formulation
For many loss functions, the DRO problem has a closed-form dual:
```
inf_λ≥0 { λε + E_P[sup_q L(q) - λ * c(q, p)] }
```

Where c(·,·) is the transport cost.

### Use Cases
- When PPC (posterior predictive checks) fail, switch to DRO
- When Wasserstein drift is detected, tighten thresholds
- Produces conservative 'don't kill unless safe' decisions

### Integration with Decision Core
- Compute standard expected loss E_P[L]
- When drift/misspecification detected, compute DRO worst-case
- Use DRO bound as conservative gate
- Gate prevents kills that are risky under distribution shift

### Ambiguity Radius Selection
- Calibrate ε from historical drift magnitudes
- Adapt based on model confidence
- Larger ε when more uncertain

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Wasserstein DRO computation implemented
- [ ] Closed-form dual used where available
- [ ] Integration with drift detection triggers
- [ ] Conservative gating when DRO activated
- [ ] Ambiguity radius configurable

## Dependencies
- Wasserstein distance computation (see 4.24)
- Posterior predictive checks (4.41)
- Phase 5 (decision theory)

## Technical Notes
- Use analytic dual bounds where available
- Conservative approximations when exact is intractable
- DRO is a gate, not replacement for main posterior","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T08:56:21.017350660Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T10:46:48.129868376Z","closed_at":"2026-01-15T10:46:48.129868376Z","close_reason":"duplicate (canonical: process_triage-6a1)","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-0gtc","depends_on_id":"process_triage-p15","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:54Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-0ij","title":"Implement Bayes factor computation","description":"## Purpose
Compute **Bayes factors / marginal likelihood ratios** for `pt`’s closed-form Bayesian models, and expose them in a way that supports:
- evidence ledger explainability (top contributors)
- MDL/code-length interpretation (“evidence as compression”)
- e-value based multiplicity control (`process_triage-sqe`)

This bead is not “posterior computation” (`process_triage-wb3` / `process_triage-e48`). It is the *evidence ratio* machinery that helps explain and safely aggregate decisions.

## Definitions
For two hypotheses H1 and H0:
- `BF_{1,0}(x) = P(x | H1) / P(x | H0)`
- `log BF = log P(x|H1) - log P(x|H0)`

In `pt`, H0/H1 may be class-conditional models or action-conditional models.

### Conjugate marginal likelihoods
Because we use conjugate models, `P(x|H)` is often available in closed form.
Examples:
- Beta–Binomial marginal for occupancy counts
- Beta–Bernoulli marginal for binary activity
- Dirichlet–Multinomial marginal for categorical features
- Gamma survival / hazard likelihood terms for runtime evidence

## Bayes Factors as e-values (important for Plan §5.8)
A likelihood ratio or marginal likelihood ratio can serve as an e-value:
- `e(x) = m_1(x) / m_0(x)`
- under H0, `E[e(x)] = 1`

This is the bridge that allows:
- optional stopping / repeated scans
- FDR control using e-values (`process_triage-sqe`)

Implementation requirement:
- expose per-candidate e-values (at least for abandoned-vs-useful comparisons) in a stable, auditable format.

## MDL / Code-Length Interpretation (Plan §4.4)
Define code length under hypothesis H:
- `L_Bayes(x|H) = -log ∫ p(x|θ,H) π(θ|H) dθ = -log m_H(x)`

Then:
- `log BF_{1,0}(x) = -L_Bayes(x|H1) - ( -L_Bayes(x|H0) )`
- `ΔL = L_Bayes(x|H0) - L_Bayes(x|H1)`

Expose `ΔL` in:
- nats (default)
- bits (`ΔL / ln 2`) for UI/report friendliness

## Scope
### 1) Stable API for BF computation
Provide functions (conceptual):
- `log_marginal_likelihood(model_id, data, hyperparams) -> f64`
- `log_bayes_factor(h1_model, h0_model, data) -> f64`
- `e_value_from_log_bf(log_bf) -> f64` (with overflow-safe handling)

### 2) Model family support (minimum early set)
- Beta–Bernoulli
- Beta–Binomial
- Dirichlet–Multinomial
- Gamma runtime terms (density vs survival as configured)

### 3) Evidence strength labeling (for UI)
Provide a deterministic mapping of |logBF| to labels (e.g., Jeffreys-style), but ensure:
- the raw numeric logBF is always available
- labels are presentation only

### 4) Numerics
- Always compute in log domain.
- Clamp/handle extreme logBF so we don’t overflow when converting to e-values.

## Output Contract
For each evidence term (and optionally for aggregated comparisons), produce:
- `log_bf`
- `bf` or `e_value` (when safe)
- `delta_bits`
- `direction` (supports H1 or H0)

This output feeds:
- `process_triage-myq` (evidence ledger)
- `process_triage-sqe` (FDR selection)
- galaxy-brain report cards

## Logging Requirements
- Log model_id + term_id + logBF + delta_bits.
- Do not log sensitive raw strings.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements log-domain marginal likelihoods for the required conjugate families.
- [ ] Produces stable logBF outputs for fixture inputs.
- [ ] Converts logBF → e-values in an overflow-safe way.
- [ ] Provides ΔL in bits for explainability.

## Test Plan
### Unit (golden)
- Known closed-form cases:
  - BF=1 when H0==H1
  - symmetric swap property: `logBF(H1,H0) = -logBF(H0,H1)`
- Compare Beta–Binomial and Beta–Bernoulli marginal likelihoods to reference computations.

### Property
- Non-negativity of e-values
- Symmetry of logBF swap

### Integration
- Use a fixture posterior/ledger pipeline and verify:
  - evidence ledger lists top terms consistent with logBF ordering

### Logging
- On failure, tests print model parameters + logBF + Δbits.

All 38 bayes_factor tests + all model-specific tests pass.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-0ij","depends_on_id":"process_triage-00b","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:54Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-0ij","depends_on_id":"process_triage-22q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:54Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-0ij","depends_on_id":"process_triage-3ot","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:54Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-0ij","depends_on_id":"process_triage-5s5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:54Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-0ij","depends_on_id":"process_triage-m99","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:54Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-0ij","depends_on_id":"process_triage-nao","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:54Z","created_by":"import"}]}
